title: Stop Disaster Price Gouging Act
plain_summary: This bill makes it illegal for businesses to raise prices too much during emergencies and disasters. Companies cannot increase prices by more than ten percent on important things people need, unless their own costs went up or they had already planned to change prices. If businesses break this rule, they can be sued by the government or regular people and have to pay fines up to twenty five thousand dollars for each time they did it wrong.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Stop Disaster Price Gouging Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Prohibition on Price Gouging
sections.text: This section creates rules about raising prices during disasters. When the President declares a major disaster or emergency in an area, businesses in that area cannot raise prices too much. For essential goods like food and water, hotel rooms, and rental homes, businesses cannot raise prices more than ten percent above what they charged the day before the disaster was declared. This rule lasts for thirty days. For repair and construction services, the ten percent limit lasts for one hundred eighty days. Businesses also cannot charge more than fifty percent above their actual costs for these items and services during the first thirty days after a disaster, if they were not charging those high prices before the disaster happened. There are some exceptions to these rules. Businesses can raise prices more than ten percent if their suppliers raised prices on them, but only if the increase is not more than ten percent above their usual costs plus their normal markup. Price increases due to tariffs or national trade policies are also allowed. Hotels can raise rates for seasonal adjustments that were already scheduled. Landlords can raise rent more than ten percent if they need to pay for major repairs beyond normal maintenance, or if a rent increase was already agreed to in a contract before the disaster. The Federal Trade Commission will enforce these rules. Breaking these rules counts as an unfair or deceptive business practice. The Commission can use all the same powers it normally has to stop unfair business practices. People who break these rules face the penalties described later in this section. State attorneys general can also sue businesses that break these rules on behalf of their state's residents. They can ask courts to stop the illegal pricing, force businesses to follow the law, get money back for residents who were overcharged, or seek other remedies. Before filing a lawsuit, state officials must notify the Federal Trade Commission. If the federal government is already suing the same business for the same violations, the state cannot file its own lawsuit until the federal case is finished. Individual people who are hurt by illegal price gouging can also sue in state or federal court. They can ask the court to stop the illegal pricing and get money back for their losses. If the court finds that a business willfully broke the law, it can award up to three times the actual damages. People who win these lawsuits can get their legal costs and attorney fees paid by the business that broke the law. People have two years from when they discovered the violation to file a lawsuit. These lawsuits do not prevent people from using other legal remedies that might be available. Businesses that break these rules can be fined up to twenty five thousand dollars for each violation. However, the total fines against one business for related violations cannot exceed twenty five thousand dollars. These fine amounts will be adjusted each year based on inflation using the Consumer Price Index. Money collected from these fines will go into a fund to help communities affected by disasters. This section defines important terms. The Commission means the Federal Trade Commission. Essential consumer goods and services are things people need to survive and recover during and after disasters. This includes food and drinks for people and animals, emergency supplies like water and generators and flashlights and radios and batteries and candles and blankets and soap and diapers and temporary shelters and tape and toiletries and plywood and nails and hammers, medical supplies like prescription and non prescription medicines and bandages and gauze and alcohol and antibacterial products, home heating oil, building materials like lumber and construction tools and windows, transportation services, freight services, storage services, and gasoline and other motor fuels. This law does not override state laws unless those state laws conflict with this federal law.
